items.subject: psychology
items.topic: the peak-end rule in how people remember experiences
items.transcript: The peak-end rule describes a curious pattern in how people remember experiences, and it comes from research by the psychologist Daniel Kahneman. When we recall an event, a vacation, a medical procedure, even a conversation, we don't average every moment we lived through. Instead, memory relies heavily on two points: the most intense moment, whether pleasant or painful, and how the experience ended. Duration barely matters at all, a finding researchers call duration neglect. Kahneman demonstrated this with a study of patients undergoing colonoscopies. Some patients received the standard procedure. Others received the same procedure, but the doctor left the instrument in place a bit longer at the end, at a much lower intensity, so the discomfort tapered off gradually instead of stopping abruptly. Logically, that longer procedure should have felt worse, since there was more total discomfort overall. But patients who experienced the gentler ending rated the whole procedure as less unpleasant, and they were more willing to return for a follow-up. Their memory wasn't built from the sum of every uncomfortable second. It was built from the peak of pain and the final impression. This matters well beyond a doctor's office. Consider a vacation with one spectacular day and a smooth departure, compared with a vacation that was pleasant throughout but ended with a canceled flight. People often remember the first more fondly, even if the second contained more good moments in total. So when a business cares about how customers will remember an experience, closely managing the intensity of the ending can matter more than optimizing every single step along the way.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: A psychological pattern in how people remember past experiences
options.B: A specific study on why colonoscopy patients return for follow-ups
options.C: How people choose which souvenirs to buy on vacation
options.D: The medical risks associated with colonoscopy procedures

questions.explanation: 正确答案A概括了讲座主旨：人们记忆过往经历时依赖的一种心理规律，即峰终定律。B只提到讲座中用来举例的一项具体研究，属too_narrow，只抓住一个细节。C借用了vacation这个词，但讲座并未讨论如何挑选纪念品，属wrong_topic。D使用了colonoscopy这一关键词，但讲座讨论的不是医疗风险，而是记忆规律，属surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did Kahneman's colonoscopy study find?

options.A: Patients with a gentler ending experienced a shorter overall procedure
options.B: Patients with a gentler ending rated the whole procedure as less unpleasant
options.C: Patients who returned for follow-ups had less severe conditions
options.D: Patients with the gentler ending rated the procedure as more unpleasant

questions.explanation: 正确答案B与讲座一致：结尾更温和的患者认为整个过程不那么令人不快。A说这类患者经历了更短的过程，但讲座说恰恰相反，过程被延长了，属wrong_detail。C说复诊患者病情较轻，讲座并未提及，属topic_adjacent。D把结论完全说反，讲座指出结尾温和的患者感觉更不痛苦，而非更痛苦，属reversed_logic。

questions.stem: Why does the professor describe the colonoscopy study in detail?

options.A: To criticize hospitals for performing unnecessary procedures
options.B: To argue that colonoscopies should always add a gentler ending
options.C: To provide concrete evidence for how the peak-end rule works
options.D: To describe how doctors adjusted the timing of an instrument

questions.explanation: 正确答案C点明了这项研究的作用：为峰终定律提供具体证据。A把目的曲解为批评医院做不必要的检查，属wrong_purpose。B把教授没有表达的主张强加给他，教授并未建议所有结肠镜检查都该延长，属over_inference。D只是复述实验操作的字面内容，而非解释举例的用意，属literal_restatement。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about businesses that care about how customers remember a service?

options.A: Every business should ignore all customer feedback except endings
options.B: The overall duration of a service is what customers remember best
options.C: Companies should survey customers immediately after every purchase
options.D: Managing the ending of an experience can matter more than its length

questions.explanation: 正确答案D符合讲座结尾的暗示：管理好体验结尾的强度，可能比优化每一个环节更重要。A把观点推向极端，说应忽视除结尾外的一切反馈，讲座并未如此断言，属over_generalization。B把方向说反，讲座强调的是duration neglect，即持续时间并不是记忆的关键，属reversed_logic。C提出一个看似合理但讲座未提及的建议，属topic_adjacent。

items.subject: biology
items.topic: the role of bioluminescence in deep-sea organisms
items.transcript: In the deep ocean, sunlight disappears entirely below about a thousand meters, yet the water down there is far from dark. Most of the light comes from the animals themselves, through a process called bioluminescence. Bioluminescence is the production of light by a living organism, driven by a chemical reaction between a light-emitting molecule called luciferin and an enzyme called luciferase. When these two react, energy is released mostly as light instead of heat, which is remarkably efficient compared with something like a light bulb. Deep-sea creatures put this light to several different uses. The anglerfish is probably the most familiar example. It dangles a glowing lure just above its mouth, and smaller fish, drawn to what looks like an easy meal, swim close enough to be caught. Other species use light defensively. Certain squid, when threatened, release a glowing cloud instead of ordinary ink, confusing a predator long enough for the squid to escape into the darkness. Some tiny crustaceans called ostracods use light for an entirely different purpose: courtship. Males release precisely timed pulses of blue light while swimming in set patterns, and females identify potential mates by the rhythm of these light displays. So the same basic chemical trick serves hunting, defense, and finding a mate, depending on the species. Researchers estimate that the majority of animals living below two hundred meters can produce their own light in some form, making bioluminescence one of the most common survival strategies on the planet, even though it remains almost invisible to us on the surface.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How anglerfish use a glowing lure to catch prey
options.B: How deep-sea organisms produce and use light for survival
options.C: How sunlight is absorbed at different ocean depths
options.D: The chemical composition of luciferin found in light bulbs

questions.explanation: 正确答案B概括主旨：深海生物如何产生并利用光来生存。A只提到讲座中的一个例子，鮟鱇鱼的诱饵，属too_narrow。C讨论阳光在不同深度的吸收情况，讲座并未涉及，属wrong_topic。D借用了luciferin和light bulb这些词，但把它们错误地联系在一起，讲座只是用灯泡做效率对比，属surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention the ostracods' timed light pulses?

options.A: To explain how ostracods avoid being eaten by predators
options.B: To argue that all deep-sea mating relies on light signals
options.C: To illustrate a courtship use of bioluminescence beyond hunting or defense
options.D: To describe blue light pulses timed during swimming patterns

questions.explanation: 正确答案C点明这个例子的用途：说明生物发光除了捕食和防御外，还可用于求偶。A误以为目的是解释介形虫如何躲避天敌，属wrong_purpose。B把观点推向绝对，说所有深海求偶都依赖光信号，讲座并未如此断言，属over_inference。D只是复述蓝光脉冲和游动模式的字面细节，而非解释举例的用意，属literal_restatement。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what happens when luciferin and luciferase react?

options.A: The reaction occurs between luciferin and a molecule called melatonin
options.B: The reaction generates heat that helps organisms survive cold water
options.C: The reaction releases most of its energy as heat instead of light
options.D: The reaction releases energy mostly as light instead of heat

questions.explanation: 正确答案D与讲座一致：荧光素与荧光素酶反应释放的能量主要是光而非热。A把荧光素酶换成了讲座中没有出现过的褪黑素，改变了关键事实，属wrong_detail。B说反应产生的热能帮助生物抵御寒冷，讲座未提及此用途，属topic_adjacent。C把光和热的比例完全说反，属reversed_logic。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about bioluminescence in the deep ocean?

options.A: It functions as a widespread survival and communication strategy
options.B: Nearly every organism on Earth relies on producing its own light
options.C: It is rare among organisms living below two hundred meters
options.D: It helps scientists track the migration patterns of ocean fish

questions.explanation: 正确答案A符合讲座结尾的暗示：生物发光是一种广泛存在的生存与交流策略。B把范围从深海生物扩大到地球上几乎所有生物，属over_generalization。C与讲座直接相反，讲座说两百米以下大多数动物都能发光，而非罕见，属reversed_logic。D提出一个看似合理却未被讲座提及的科研用途，属topic_adjacent。

items.subject: astronomy
items.topic: why some moons in our solar system may harbor liquid water
items.transcript: For decades, the search for liquid water beyond Earth focused mainly on Mars. But some of the most promising candidates in the solar system turned out to be much farther out: icy moons orbiting Jupiter and Saturn. Europa, one of Jupiter's moons, is covered in a shell of ice, yet evidence strongly suggests a vast ocean of liquid water lies beneath that frozen surface. What keeps that water from freezing solid, this far from the sun? The answer is tidal heating. As Europa orbits Jupiter, the planet's massive gravity stretches and squeezes the moon slightly with each orbit, and that repeated flexing generates internal friction, which produces heat. It's a bit like bending a paperclip back and forth until it warms up. This heat is enough to keep a liquid ocean beneath the ice, insulated from the extreme cold of space. Saturn's moon Enceladus offers even more direct evidence. Spacecraft have observed geysers of water vapor and ice particles erupting from cracks near its south pole, spraying material directly into space. Probes flown through these plumes have detected salts and organic molecules, strongly suggesting the plume originates from a subsurface ocean in contact with rock. That combination, liquid water, heat, and chemical ingredients, is exactly what many astrobiologists consider necessary for life as we understand it. Neither moon receives much warmth from the sun directly. Their oceans exist because of gravitational forces rather than sunlight, which means the conditions once thought necessary for habitability may be far more common across the solar system than researchers previously assumed.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: The geysers observed erupting from Enceladus's south pole
options.B: The search for liquid water on the surface of Mars
options.C: Why tidal heating allows some icy moons to hold liquid water
options.D: How paperclips generate heat when bent back and forth

questions.explanation: 正确答案C概括主旨：潮汐加热如何使某些冰冷卫星得以保有液态水。A只提到讲座中的一个细节，土卫二南极的间歇泉，属too_narrow。B讨论火星表面液态水的搜寻，那只是讲座开头提到的对比背景，并非本讲座重点，属wrong_topic。D借用了讲座中回形针的比喻，但讲座并不是讲回形针如何发热，属surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what causes the internal heat inside Europa?

options.A: Volcanic activity beneath the icy crust generates the internal heat
options.B: Radioactive decay in the moon's core produces most of the heat
options.C: Europa's orbit shields it from Jupiter's gravitational pull
options.D: Gravitational flexing from Jupiter creates friction inside the moon

questions.explanation: 正确答案D与讲座一致：木星引力反复拉伸挤压木卫二，摩擦生热。A把内部热源说成火山活动，讲座并未提及，属wrong_detail。B提到放射性衰变产热，这在天文学中合理但讲座未提，属topic_adjacent。C说木卫二的轨道使其免受木星引力影响，与讲座描述的机制完全相反，属reversed_logic。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what have spacecraft detected in the plumes erupting from Enceladus?

options.A: Salts and organic molecules suggesting an ocean in contact with rock
options.B: Pure water vapor with no dissolved salts or minerals
options.C: Traces of methane gas similar to that found on Mars
options.D: Evidence that the plumes come from surface ice rather than a subsurface ocean

questions.explanation: 正确答案A与讲座一致：探测器在羽流中检测到盐分和有机分子，暗示羽流源自与岩石接触的地下海洋。B说羽流只是不含盐分的纯水蒸气，改变了关键事实，属wrong_detail。C提到甲烷，这是与火星探测相关但本讲座未提的细节，属topic_adjacent。D把结论完全说反，讲座指出证据支持地下海洋，而非地表冰层，属reversed_logic。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about conditions for habitability in the solar system?

options.A: Every icy moon in the solar system definitely harbors life
options.B: They may be more common than scientists once assumed
options.C: Liquid water can only exist on planets close to the sun
options.D: Future missions should prioritize searching for Martian fossils

questions.explanation: 正确答案B符合讲座结尾的暗示：适宜居住的条件可能比科学家过去认为的更普遍。A把观点推向绝对，断言每颗冰卫星都一定有生命，讲座并未如此断言，属over_generalization。C与讲座直接相反，讲座恰恰说明远离太阳的卫星也能凭借潮汐加热保有液态水，属reversed_logic。D提出一个看似合理但讲座未提及的建议，属topic_adjacent。

items.subject: music
items.topic: how recording technology changed the way music was composed
items.transcript: Recording technology didn't just capture music. It changed how composers approached the act of composing in the first place. Before magnetic tape became widely available in the late nineteen forties, a piece of music existed as a score, and any recording was, at best, a faithful snapshot of a single performance. Tape editing broke that assumption. A composer, or an engineer working closely with one, could physically cut a reel of tape with a razor blade and splice sections back together in a different order, combine takes recorded days apart, or reverse a passage so it played backward. Pierre Schaeffer, working in Paris in the late nineteen forties, pushed this further with an approach called musique concrete. Rather than writing notes for musicians to perform, Schaeffer composed directly with recorded sounds, train whistles, kitchen noises, fragments of instruments, splicing and layering them on tape until the finished piece existed only as a specific arrangement of recordings. No score could fully represent it, and no ensemble could recreate it live from notation alone. That idea, composing with the recording medium itself rather than for eventual performance, spread well beyond experimental circles. Popular musicians later borrowed similar techniques, splicing together the best sections from multiple takes or reversing a guitar passage for a particular effect. The finished song became something assembled in the studio rather than something merely documented there. This shift raised a genuinely difficult question for musicologists: if a piece cannot be performed live in its original form, is it still music in the traditional sense, or something closer to a new art form built around the recording process itself?

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: The specific splicing techniques Pierre Schaeffer used in Paris
options.B: The invention of magnetic tape as a storage medium
options.C: How kitchen noises were recorded for use in film soundtracks
options.D: How recording technology transformed the process of composing music

questions.explanation: 正确答案D概括主旨：录音技术如何改变了作曲这一过程本身。A只讲了皮埃尔·舍费尔在巴黎使用的具体剪辑手法，属too_narrow。B讨论磁带作为存储介质的发明，那只是背景，不是讲座重点，属wrong_topic。C借用了kitchen noises这一词汇，但讲座并未讨论电影配乐，属surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did Pierre Schaeffer do differently in musique concrete?

options.A: He composed directly with recorded sounds instead of writing notation
options.B: He recorded only orchestral instruments rather than everyday sounds
options.C: He taught composition techniques at a Paris conservatory
options.D: He insisted that every piece must be reproducible by a live ensemble

questions.explanation: 正确答案A与讲座一致：舍费尔直接用录制的声音进行创作，而非为乐手写乐谱。B说他只录制管弦乐器而非日常声响，改变了关键事实，讲座提到的是火车汽笛和厨房噪音等日常声音，属wrong_detail。C提到他在巴黎音乐学院任教，讲座未提及，属topic_adjacent。D把结论完全说反，讲座明确指出没有乐团能仅凭乐谱重现这类作品，属reversed_logic。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention that no ensemble could recreate Schaeffer's piece live from notation alone?

options.A: To criticize orchestras for refusing to perform experimental works
options.B: To show that the recording itself had become the actual composition
options.C: To argue that live performance had become entirely obsolete
options.D: To note that Schaeffer's piece could not be written as a score

questions.explanation: 正确答案B点明了这一说法的用意：录音本身已经成为真正的作品。A把目的曲解为批评乐团拒绝演出实验音乐，讲座并无此意，属wrong_purpose。C把观点推向极端，说现场演出已完全过时，讲座并未如此断言，属over_inference。D只是复述这句话的字面内容，而非解释为什么要提到它，属literal_restatement。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about songs assembled through splicing and studio techniques?

options.A: They are no longer considered music by any listener today
options.B: They are always a more accurate document of a live performance
options.C: They raise questions about whether they fit traditional definitions of music
options.D: They require composers to have formal training as sound engineers

questions.explanation: 正确答案C符合讲座结尾提出的问题：这类作品是否仍属于传统意义上的音乐，引发了音乐学者的疑问。A把观点推向绝对，说没有听众认为它们是音乐，讲座并未如此断言，属over_generalization。B与讲座相反，讲座强调这类歌曲是在录音室里组装出来的，而非对现场演出的忠实记录，属reversed_logic。D提出一个看似合理但讲座未提及的要求，属topic_adjacent。
